reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: All four engine variants are covered with working code examples and per-variant tradeoffs — no variant is treated as an afterthought.
FeatureComparisonTable gives readers a clear, scannable side-by-side engine comparison before diving into subsections.
'When to use' and 'When NOT to use' are correctly placed as peer H2 sections with concrete, opinionated guidance.
The connectTo section pairs the Stacktape config example with an actual application handler snippet, closing the loop from config to running code.
FAQ covers all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS use-case, comparison) across 10 well-written Q&A pairs.
Progressive structure: minimal basic example near the top, complexity layers below — matches how the target audience reads docs.
Explicit destructive-action warnings (masterUserName change replaces DB, deletionProtection must be disabled before delete) protect first-time users from data loss.
Pricing context is present near the top and is properly sourced with '~' approximations.
problems: CRITICAL: Version `16.6` is used in every Aurora Serverless v2 code example (basic example, Aurora Serverless v2 subsection, accessibility example, connecting from workloads example, alarms example). Per versions.json, `16.6` does NOT appear in the `aurora-postgresql-serverless-v2` supported list — the highest 16.x entry is `16.11`. A user copy-pasting the very first code block on the page will get an immediate deploy failure.

The `connectTo` section lists `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING`, `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_HOST`, `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_PORT` as the injected env vars, then says `jdbcConnectionString` is only available via `$ResourceParam()`. However, the `ScriptEnvProps.connectTo` JSDoc (same source file) lists `JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING` as also injected alongside `CONNECTION_STRING`, `HOST`, `PORT`. The page incorrectly implies JDBC requires a manual directive.

The 'Accessibility' section heading is likely to confuse first-time developers who associate 'accessibility' with a11y (WCAG). A reader skimming headings may skip the section or not know it controls network-level connection restrictions — arguably the most important security configuration on the page.

The engine types section is the largest section (four subsections, four code blocks) and appears before foundational configuration topics (Credentials, Networking/Accessibility). A first-time user follows the page top-to-bottom and must read through all four engine variants before reaching 'how to store my password safely' and 'how to restrict network access'.
mandatoryFixes: Fix Aurora Serverless v2 version in all code examples: replace `version: '16.6'` with a version actually present in the `aurora-postgresql-serverless-v2` list in versions.json (e.g. `'16.11'` or `'15.15'`). This affects the 'Basic example' block at the top of the page plus at least four subsequent blocks. Leaving `16.6` means the primary copy-paste target example fails at deploy time for the target persona.
optionalImprovements: Add `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING` to the auto-injected vars listed in the connectTo section (the `ScriptEnvProps.connectTo` JSDoc lists it alongside CONNECTION_STRING, HOST, PORT). At minimum, move the `jdbcConnectionString` mention from the '$ResourceParam() only' sentence to the injected-vars list.

Rename the 'Accessibility' H2 to 'Network access' or 'Connectivity' to avoid the a11y association. The word 'accessibility' is accurate (it maps to the source property name) but immediately confusing for the target audience.

Consider reordering: move 'Credentials' and 'Network access' above the 'Engine types' section. First-time users need to know 'store my password safely' and 'who can reach my DB' before they need the full four-variant engine breakdown.

In the Aurora Serverless v2 subsection, the code example is nearly identical to the 'Basic example' at the top of the page. Use this subsection's example to demonstrate an additional feature (e.g. `serverlessReadersCount: 2`) so it adds value beyond the basic example.

The Aurora Serverless v1 subsection warns against using it but still provides a full code example. Consider making the v1 example a collapsed/minimal snippet or moving it after a stronger 'legacy only' callout to reduce the chance that first-time users choose v1 by mistake.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Comprehensive engine-family coverage: all four families (RDS, Aurora, Aurora Serverless v2, Aurora Serverless v1) get working code examples, tradeoffs, and when-to-use guidance — equal depth across variants.
Comparison table for engine selection is well-structured and citable; covers scaling model, HA, supported engines, and rough cost in one view.

Factual accuracy on defaults: masterUserName ('db_master_user'), automatedBackupRetentionDays (1), minCapacity/maxCapacity defaults, Aurora Serverless v1 min-capacity powers-of-2 constraint, Aurora Serverless accessibility restriction — all match the source types.

Accessibility section is unusually good for a resource page: four modes are defined precisely, tradeoffs and production recommendation are explicit, and the `forceDisablePublicIp` Aurora creation-time constraint is called out.
No forbidden patterns: all Stacktape config examples use `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs>` with TypeScript; no fenced yaml resources, no StacktapeConfig type import, no hand-authored YAML tabs.
The `masterUserName` data-loss warning and credential special-character restriction are both present and correctly scoped — readers won't be blindsided post-deploy.
FAQ spans all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS-service use-case, choosing/comparison) and has exactly 10 entries.
Pricing context is correctly attributed to the generated pricing source, uses '~' approximations, and distinguishes RDS vs Aurora vs Serverless v2 billing models.
problems: FACTUAL ERROR (high severity): Every Aurora Serverless v2 code example uses `version: '16.6'`. The provided `@generated/db-engine-versions/versions.json` shows that `aurora-postgresql-serverless-v2` does NOT include version `16.6` — the 16.x versions for serverless-v2 jump from 15.x directly to `16.10` and `16.11`. A reader copying any of these examples would receive a deployment error. The non-serverless `aurora-postgresql` engine does include `16.6`, so this appears to be an engine-type mix-up. Affected examples: basic example, Aurora Serverless v2 deep-dive, accessibility example, connecting example, alarms example.

The 'Connecting from workloads' section states that `jdbcConnectionString` is only available via `$ResourceParam()`. This is accurate for workload `connectTo` (per `ResourceAccessProps` JSDoc which lists `CONNECTION_STRING`, `HOST`, `PORT` for RelationalDatabase). However, the source `ScriptEnvProps` JSDoc explicitly lists `JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING` as auto-injected when scripts use `connectTo`. The page's FAQ answer on migrations references lifecycle hooks/scripts, yet never tells the reader that `JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING` is injected there. This creates a silent gap for users migrating with JDBC-based tools (e.g., Flyway, Liquibase).
mandatoryFixes: Replace `version: '16.6'` with `version: '16.10'` (or '16.11') in all five Aurora Serverless v2 code examples. Per `@generated/db-engine-versions/versions.json`, `aurora-postgresql-serverless-v2` only supports 13.x, 14.x, 15.x, 16.10, and 16.11 — version 16.6 is absent from that list. Using 16.6 will cause a deployment error for any reader who copies the examples.
optionalImprovements: Rename the 'Accessibility' H2 section to 'Network access' or 'Access control'. 'Accessibility' is strongly associated with web a11y (WCAG) in developer contexts; 'Network access' immediately signals what the section controls.

In the 'Connecting from workloads' section, add a one-sentence note that when using `connectTo` inside a `local-script` (for migrations, seed scripts, etc.), `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING` and, for Aurora, `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_READER_JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING` are also injected — per the `ScriptEnvProps` JSDoc. This matters for JDBC-based migration tools.

All Aurora engines (Aurora, Serverless v2, Serverless v1) default `dbName` to `'defdb'` per the source types. A one-line callout near each engine's example would help users who configure ORMs or Prisma datasource URLs where the database name matters (e.g., Convex's strict URL parsing gotcha in the project memory illustrates this class of problem).

The logging code example only demonstrates PostgreSQL `engineSpecificOptions`. A brief prose note listing the MySQL equivalent options (`server_audit_events`, `long_query_time`) would complete the section without a second code example.

The FAQ answer on migrations mentions `local-script-with-bastion-tunneling` as the path for VPC databases, but doesn't link to the deployment-script or hooks docs directly — adding concrete links would make it actionable.

Consider a one-sentence callout in the 'High availability and read replicas' section about RDS Proxy or connection pooling for Lambda-heavy workloads — Lambda's concurrent invocations can exhaust database connections quickly, which is a common production failure mode not addressed anywhere on the page.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: All four engine variants (RDS, Aurora, Aurora Serverless v2, Aurora Serverless v1) receive per-variant code examples, decision guidance, and roughly equal depth — union-type coverage discipline is fully satisfied.
The engine comparison `<FeatureComparisonTable>` is an excellent scannable decision tool that covers scaling, HA, read replicas, pause behavior, cost, and engine support in one place.

Environment variable names are correctly derived from the example resource name `mainDatabase` (e.g., `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING`) and only the params enumerated in the `connectTo` JSDoc are claimed as auto-injected.
All pricing figures are sourced from the provided pricing data and properly qualified with `~`, including the per-instance-class table in the Standard RDS subsection.
Decision support is strong throughout: 'When to use,' 'When NOT to use,' and per-section tradeoff guidance (multi-AZ cost doubling, Aurora Serverless v2 for dev/staging, accessibility mode table).
The 10-question FAQ spans all three required categories — Stacktape-specific, AWS service use-case, and choosing/comparison — with well-grounded, actionable answers.
No forbidden patterns: no fenced YAML `resources:` blocks, no `StacktapeConfig` typed style, no fenced TypeScript containing `defineConfig`, no hand-authored YAML tabs.

The `connectTo` section is careful to document only the env vars that appear in the source `ResourceAccessProps` JSDoc and directs readers to `$ResourceParam()` for additional params, avoiding unsupported negative claims.
The `<Warning>` callout for `masterUserName` change is placed exactly where the risk occurs, not buried in a footnote.
The `Accessibility` section table (mode vs who can connect vs best-for) is immediately actionable and saves the reader from reading four paragraphs of prose.
problems: The H2 heading 'Accessibility' is likely to mislead readers who associate the term with web accessibility (WCAG, screen readers, a11y). A name like 'Network access' or 'Connectivity' would be unambiguous and more scannable.

The class names `AuroraServerlessV2EnginePostgresql`, `AuroraServerlessV2EngineMysql`, `AuroraServerlessEnginePostgresql`, and `AuroraServerlessEngineMysql` are used in code blocks. The writer instructions state 'Database engine classes start with `RdsEngine` or `AuroraEngine`' — these names start with `AuroraServerless`, not `AuroraEngine`. The page does mitigate this by calling out each class name explicitly in prose (the recommended pattern), and the code-block-validator is authoritative; if it does not flag them, the classes exist. This is a low risk, but reviewers should verify the validator result for these four names.

The claim 'Resume time can take 30 seconds or more' for Aurora Serverless v1 pause/resume appears in the body text without source grounding. The source files confirm the pause feature but not the 30-second figure. This is a well-known AWS fact but is framed in the body (not FAQ) and could draw a factual-verifier flag.
optionalImprovements: Rename the '## Accessibility' H2 to '## Network access' or '## Connectivity' to avoid confusion with web accessibility terminology.

Move or qualify the 'Resume time can take 30 seconds or more' claim about Aurora Serverless v1 — either attribute it explicitly as an AWS-side behavior ('AWS Aurora Serverless v1 can take 30 seconds or more to resume...') or move it to the FAQ where well-known AWS facts are explicitly permitted without strict source grounding.
Shorten '## High availability and read replicas' to '## High availability' — read replicas are a sub-concern within HA and the longer heading edges toward sentence-form.

Add a one-liner in the Aurora, Aurora Serverless v1, and Aurora Serverless v2 subsections noting that the initial database name defaults to 'defdb' — this is a practical gotcha (e.g., when constructing a connection string in scripts) that the API reference documents but the prose does not surface.

The 'Dev mode' section is the shortest section on the page (two sentences plus a link). Consider adding one sentence explaining what the local Docker emulation provides — e.g., which engine flavour it emulates, or whether schema migrations run against it during `stacktape dev` — so readers can decide whether `remote: true` applies to their situation without leaving the page.

Consider linking 'bastion host' on its first mention in the Connectivity section to '/resources/security/bastion-host' — it is already linked in the FAQ and the 'Connecting from workloads' section but the Accessibility section mentions VPC-restricted databases without that link.

issues.statement: Multiple Aurora Serverless v2 code examples use `version: '16.6'` — the Basic example, Aurora Serverless v2 section, Accessibility section, Connecting from workloads section, and Alarms section all specify this version for `AuroraServerlessV2EnginePostgresql`.
issues.reason: Version `16.6` is NOT in the `aurora-postgresql-serverless-v2` supported versions list. The supported versions for that engine type are 16.11, 16.10, 15.15, 15.14, 15.13, 15.12, 15.10, 14.20, etc. Version 16.6 IS valid for standard `aurora-postgresql` and RDS `postgres`, but not for Serverless v2. Readers who copy these examples will get deployment failures.
issues.suggestedFix: Change `version: '16.6'` to `version: '16.10'` (or `'16.11'`) in all Aurora Serverless v2 examples. These are the latest 16.x versions available for `aurora-postgresql-serverless-v2`.

issues.statement: The FeatureComparisonTable shows Aurora Serverless v1 scaling as 'Auto (1–256 ACUs)' without distinguishing the PostgreSQL minimum.
issues.reason: The source type `AuroraServerlessEngineProperties` documents the minCapacity range as: 'MySQL: 1-256 (powers of 2). PostgreSQL: 2-256 (powers of 2).' The PostgreSQL minimum is 2, not 1. The comparison table shows a single '1–256' range which is only correct for MySQL. The dedicated Aurora Serverless v1 section below does note 'PostgreSQL minimum is 2', so the information is on the page — but the table itself is inaccurate and is the most-scanned element.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the table cell to 'Auto (1–256 ACUs, PostgreSQL min 2)' or 'Auto (2–256 ACUs for PostgreSQL, 1–256 for MySQL)'.

evidence.quote: MySQL: 1-256 (powers of 2). PostgreSQL: 2-256 (powers of 2).

issues.statement: The 'Storage' section consists of a single short paragraph with no code example, no cost context, and no guidance on when to change defaults. It is the briefest substantive section on the page.
issues.reason: Storage is a real configuration lever for RDS users. The section correctly states the defaults (20 GB initial, 200 GB max) and mentions auto-scaling, but lacks: (1) a code example showing how to set custom storage values, (2) any cost signal (RDS storage costs a few cents per GB/month for gp2/gp3), (3) guidance on storage types (gp2, gp3, io1 — though these may be managed by Stacktape, the reader has no way to know). For a page that provides code examples and tradeoff analysis for every other section, this one stands out as thin.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a short code example showing `storage: { initialSize: 50, maxSize: 500 }` on an RDS engine, a sentence on storage cost magnitude, and a note that this property only applies to RDS engines (Aurora manages storage automatically, as the section already states).

issues.statement: The Aurora Serverless v2 section says the database 'scales up instantly under load' (from the minCapacity JSDoc) and the Aurora Serverless v1 Warning says v2 has 'faster scaling'.
issues.reason: The source JSDoc for AuroraServerlessV2Engine says 'Scales instantly from 0.5 to 128 ACUs' while the v1 source just says 'Scales compute capacity automatically'. The word 'instantly' in the v2 JSDoc is marketing-flavored — Aurora Serverless v2 scaling takes seconds, not zero time. The draft mostly avoids this word but the prose says 'scales up instantly' in the v2 section which could over-set expectations. This is a minor issue since the general AWS behavior is well-known.
issues.suggestedFix: Consider softening to 'scales up automatically under load' or 'scales up within seconds under load' to avoid implying zero-latency scaling.

evidence.quote: Set low (0.5) for dev/staging to minimize cost. The database scales up instantly under load.

positiveFindings: All four engine type variants (RDS, Aurora, Aurora Serverless v1, Aurora Serverless v2) are covered with equal depth — each gets a dedicated subsection, a working code example, engine type enumeration, class names, and when-to-use guidance.

Property defaults are accurately sourced throughout: masterUserName ('db_master_user'), automatedBackupRetentionDays (1), minCapacity/maxCapacity for v2 (0.5/10), storage initialSize/maxSize (20/200), logging retentionDays (90), all match the @default annotations in the .d.ts source.

All six database alarm trigger types (database-cpu-utilization, database-free-memory, database-free-storage, database-connection-count, database-read-latency, database-write-latency) and their threshold units are correctly enumerated and match the alarms.d.ts source.

Accessibility modes are accurately documented: all four modes listed with correct default ('internet'), correct Aurora Serverless restriction (only vpc or scoping-workloads-in-vpc), and correct whitelistedIps behavior per mode — all grounded in the DatabaseAccessibility source type.

connectTo environment variable claims correctly follow the ResourceAccessProps JSDoc — CONNECTION_STRING, HOST, PORT for base; READER_CONNECTION_STRING and READER_HOST for Aurora. The draft avoids making negative claims about what is NOT injected, per the documented pattern.
Pricing figures closely match the distilled pricing source: db.t4g.micro ~$12/month (source: ~$11.52), db.t3.small Aurora ~$35/month (source: ~$35.28). Appropriate use of '~' approximation.
All 20+ cross-links point to valid routes in the navigation index — no invented routes found.
The ApiReference uses the correct definitionName 'RelationalDatabaseProps' matching the page's typeName, and ReferenceableParams uses the correct resource type 'relational-database'.

The FAQ covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (migrations, custom domains, connectTo), AWS service questions (RDS cost, Aurora vs RDS, backup/restore), and choosing/comparison (Aurora Serverless v2 vs RDS, instance sizing). Exactly 10 questions as required.
The 'When NOT to use' section is correctly an H2 peer of 'When to use' — not nested as H3.
verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-structured and factually accurate across the large majority of its claims. Property defaults, alarm types, accessibility modes, connectTo env vars, and pricing all match their source files. The primary factual issue is that all Aurora Serverless v2 code examples use PostgreSQL version '16.6', which is not in the aurora-postgresql-serverless-v2 supported versions list (the closest available 16.x versions are 16.10 and 16.11). This affects 5+ copyable examples and would cause deployment failures. A secondary issue is the comparison table oversimplifying Aurora Serverless v1's capacity range. The Storage section would benefit from a code example and slightly more depth to match the quality bar set by the rest of the page. Overall the page is strong — engine variant coverage is thorough and balanced, decision support is present throughout, and cross-links are all valid.

The draft is broadly aligned with the relational-database type source and follows the required config-example style, but it has one publication-blocking configuration problem: several Aurora Serverless v2 examples use a PostgreSQL engine version that is not in the generated supported-version source. A few other claims should be narrowed to match the source more precisely.

issues.statement: Several `AuroraServerlessV2EnginePostgresql` examples set `version: '16.6'`, including the Basic example, Accessibility, Connecting from workloads, and Alarms examples.
issues.reason: The generated engine-version source for `aurora-postgresql-serverless-v2` does not include `16.6`. These examples should use a supported Aurora PostgreSQL Serverless v2 version from the generated list, such as `16.11` or `16.10`.
issues.suggestedFix: Change every `AuroraServerlessV2EnginePostgresql({ version: '16.6', ... })` example to a supported version from `@generated/db-engine-versions/versions.json`, preferably `version: '16.11'` if the code-block validator accepts it.

issues.statement: “Stacktape handles provisioning, patching, backups, and networking.”
issues.reason: The source grounds Stacktape configuration for the database resource, but it attributes patching, backups, and recovery to AWS for RDS. The current sentence over-attributes AWS-managed database operations to Stacktape.
issues.suggestedFix: Rewrite as: “Stacktape provisions and configures the database, credentials, networking rules, backups, and maintenance settings; AWS RDS or Aurora performs the managed database operations such as patching, backups, and recovery.”

evidence.quote: Choose a fixed instance size and pay hourly. AWS handles patching, backups, and recovery.

issues.statement: The engine comparison table says Aurora Serverless v1 scaling is “Auto (1–256 ACUs)”.
issues.reason: The source distinguishes MySQL and PostgreSQL capacity ranges for Aurora Serverless v1. PostgreSQL starts at 2 ACUs, not 1 ACU, so the table needs the same caveat that the later prose includes.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the table cell to “MySQL: 1–256 ACUs; PostgreSQL: 2–256 ACUs, powers of 2” or otherwise make the engine-specific minimum explicit in the table.

evidence.quote: MySQL: 1-256 (powers of 2). PostgreSQL: 2-256 (powers of 2).

issues.statement: “For new projects, use Aurora Serverless v2 instead. It has faster scaling, more granular capacity control (0.5-ACU increments vs powers of 2), and broader version support.”
issues.reason: The source supports the recommendation, faster scaling, and more granular capacity control. It does not support the comparative claim that Aurora Serverless v2 has broader version support.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove “and broader version support” unless a source file explicitly documents that comparison. Keep the supported wording about faster scaling and more granular capacity control.

evidence.quote: For new projects, use Aurora Serverless v2 instead — it has faster scaling and more granular capacity control.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: All four engine variants (standard RDS, Aurora, Aurora Serverless v2, Aurora Serverless v1) are documented with working code examples and clear per-variant tradeoffs — no variant is left as a one-liner.
The FeatureComparisonTable gives readers a quick, concrete basis for engine selection before they read individual sections.
Critical gotchas are prominently warned: changing masterUserName destroys data; special characters break connection strings; Aurora forceDisablePublicIp is creation-time only.
The 'Connecting from workloads' section provides both the Stacktape config and real application-handler code, closing the loop for the first-time user.
FAQ has exactly 10 questions, correctly spanning Stacktape-specific, AWS use-case, and comparison categories — high AEO value.
Instance-size cost table with concrete vCPU/RAM/price values makes sizing decisions concrete rather than abstract.
Property-value anchors in examples (minCapacity, maxCapacity, instanceSize, retentionDays) are explained in surrounding prose — readers know why a value is there.
Alarm types documented in a table with threshold units, matching the source union exactly; the recommendation to set up free-storage alarm on every production DB is opinionated and useful.
problems: The 'Accessibility' H2 heading is non-intuitive for the target audience; developers unfamiliar with Stacktape property names will look for 'Networking' or 'Access control' in the page TOC.

RDS Proxy is not mentioned anywhere. Lambda functions connecting to relational databases are a primary use case and frequently hit connection-limit problems at scale; RDS Proxy is the standard AWS solution. Its absence is a real gap for the audience described.

The default database name 'defdb' for Aurora engines is never surfaced. A first-time user whose app connection string hard-codes a database name will get a connection error. The basic example or the engine sections should note that `dbName` defaults to `defdb` so the reader knows what to expect.

The 'High availability and read replicas' H2 is slightly long compared to other headings and covers two distinct concepts. Consider 'High availability' with a sub-section on read replicas, or split into two H2s.
optionalImprovements: Rename H2 'Accessibility' to 'Access control' or 'Networking' — these are the terms a reader will scan for when deciding how to restrict database access.

Add a short paragraph or callout noting the default database name is 'defdb' for Aurora engines (sourced from AuroraServerlessV2EngineProperties.dbName `@default 'defdb'`). Point readers to the `dbName` property if they need a different initial database.

Add a brief mention of RDS Proxy under 'Connecting from workloads' or as a FAQ entry: Lambda functions open a new database connection per invocation, which can exhaust max_connections on busy deployments; RDS Proxy sits in front of the database and pools connections automatically.
Shorten 'High availability and read replicas' to 'High availability' and move read-replica details into a nested H3 or a separate paragraph — keeps the TOC compact.

The Aurora v2 example in 'Basic example' sets maxCapacity: 4 while the dedicated 'Aurora Serverless v2' section sets maxCapacity: 16. Pick one or explicitly note the difference so readers aren't confused by the inconsistency.

The Logging section discusses log_min_duration_statement and log_statement in detail, but does not mention that PostgreSQL logging requires `logTypes: ['postgresql']` to be set for logs to be exported. Add a one-line note directing readers to set logTypes alongside engineSpecificOptions.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: All four engine variants (Standard RDS, Aurora, Aurora Serverless v2, Aurora Serverless v1) are documented with working code examples and equivalent depth — no variant is a one-liner afterthought.
The FeatureComparisonTable early in the Engine types section gives an at-a-glance decision matrix that is highly scannable and citation-ready.

Strong opinionated guidance throughout: Aurora Serverless v2 is called out as the recommended default, Serverless v1 gets a Warning callout pointing to v2, `scoping-workloads-in-vpc` is recommended for production with a concrete consequence for skipping it.

Production-critical gotchas are prominently surfaced: the masterUserName replacement-on-change warning, the special-character password restriction, deletionProtection must be disabled before stack delete, running out of storage makes the database read-only.
The `connectTo` section closes the loop with real application code (pg Client) that readers can copy directly — not just config.
FAQ covers all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS service mechanics, comparison), and all 10 answers are grounded and practically useful.
Alarms section goes beyond 'here is the config' — it names the minimum alarm every production database should have (free-storage) and explains why.
All numeric defaults (automatedBackupRetentionDays: 1, minCapacity: 0.5, maxCapacity: 10, log_min_duration_statement: 10000, storage.initialSize: 20) are correctly sourced from the type definitions.
problems: The connectTo section states that `jdbcConnectionString` is only available via `$ResourceParam()`, implying it is not auto-injected. However, `ScriptEnvProps.connectTo` in `__helpers.d.ts` explicitly lists `JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING` (and `READER_JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING` for Aurora) as auto-injected variables for scripts. A user writing a database migration script with `connectTo` will not find the expected `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING` env var by following the page's guidance — the page's example is workload-scoped (Lambda, ResourceAccessProps) where JDBC is indeed not listed, but the prose makes a general claim that could mislead script authors.

The section heading 'Accessibility' is ambiguous in the TOC — a developer scanning for 'how do I lock down my database to the VPC' will not immediately recognize it. 'Network access' or 'Access control' would be more intuitive for quick navigation.

The page does not surface the `dbName` default of `defdb` for Aurora and Aurora Serverless engines in prose, only in the API Reference drill-down. Users migrating from RDS (where `dbName` is optional and has no default) will be surprised to find a database named `defdb` already exists on first connect to Aurora clusters.

The page omits the `hosts` referenceable parameter, which matters for cluster-aware PostgreSQL drivers (e.g., libpq's `host` multi-host syntax) connecting to Aurora. The source defines `hosts` and `readReplicaHosts` as distinct from the single `host` param.
optionalImprovements: In the `connectTo` section, add a note distinguishing workload vs script injection: 'For Lambda functions and container workloads, `connectTo` injects `CONNECTION_STRING`, `HOST`, and `PORT`. For scripts (e.g., migration scripts), `connectTo` also injects `JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING` and, for Aurora clusters, `READER_JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING` — no `$ResourceParam()` call needed in those contexts.'
Rename the 'Accessibility' H2 to 'Network access' to improve TOC discoverability for readers looking up security configuration.

Add one sentence in the Engine types section for Aurora and Aurora Serverless engines: 'The initial database is named `defdb` by default; set `dbName` to override.' This prevents a confusing first-connect experience for users expecting an empty cluster.
Mention the `hosts` referenceable parameter in the connectTo or Referenceable parameters discussion with a short explanation of when to use it (cluster-aware drivers, pgBouncer targets).

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: All four engine families (standard RDS, Aurora, Aurora Serverless v2, Aurora Serverless v1) are covered with working code examples and per-variant decision guidance, achieving genuine equal depth — each variant gets a code block, a when-to-use paragraph, and cost/tradeoff context.

The FeatureComparisonTable is accurate and grounded in source — all values (scaling range, pause support, engine support, read replica support, and pricing) can be verified in the types and pricing distill.

Pricing anchors are grounded in both the distilled pricing source and the JSDoc comments in relational-databases.d.ts. The instance size table for standard RDS (t4g.micro ~$12, t4g.medium ~$50, r6g.large ~$180) is taken directly from source JSDoc.
The opinionated recommendation (Aurora Serverless v2 for most new projects) is stated early and reinforced throughout, giving readers a clear default without obscuring alternatives.

Every code example contains fully filled-in, copy-paste runnable configurations — no placeholders. The connectTo example is paired with a realistic handler snippet using the correct env var name derived from the example resource name (mainDatabase → STP_MAIN_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING).

The accessibility section documents all four modes in a table with use-case guidance and the constraint on Aurora Serverless engines (vpc / scoping-workloads-in-vpc only) is explicitly called out and sourced.

The 10-question FAQ mixes all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (secrets, migrations, dev mode), AWS use-case (cost, backup, Aurora vs RDS mechanics), and comparison (Aurora Serverless v2 vs RDS, instance sizing). All answers are 2–4 sentences and self-contained.

The logging section documents engine-specific options (PostgresLoggingOptions, MysqlLoggingOptions) with their defaults, units, and practical guidance (setting log_min_duration_statement to 1000 ms for production).
All alarm trigger types present in RelationalDatabaseAlarmTrigger are listed in a table with threshold units, matching the source union exactly.

Property-value anchors are present for every non-obvious tunable in every code example (minCapacity, maxCapacity, version, instanceSize, multiAz, pauseAfterSeconds) with a prose explanation of what each controls and the supported alternatives.
problems: The connectTo section states that `connectTo` injects `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING`, `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_HOST`, and `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_PORT`, then claims `jdbcConnectionString` is 'available via the `$ResourceParam()` directive.' However, the `ScriptEnvProps.connectTo` JSDoc in `__helpers.d.ts` explicitly lists `CONNECTION_STRING`, `JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING`, `HOST`, `PORT` as injected env vars for RelationalDatabase — making the statement misleading. `JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING` is injected automatically, not only accessible via `$ResourceParam()`.

The same JSDoc states that for Aurora clusters, `READER_CONNECTION_STRING`, `READER_JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING`, and `READER_HOST` are injected. The connectTo section only mentions `READER_CONNECTION_STRING` and `READER_HOST`, silently omitting `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_READER_JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING`.
optionalImprovements: In the 'Connecting from workloads' section, add `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING` to the list of injected env vars (it is injected per ScriptEnvProps JSDoc) and add `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_READER_JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING` for Aurora clusters. Then narrow the `$ResourceParam()` aside to only cover params that are genuinely not injected (readerPort, readReplicaHosts, dbName, etc.).

Add a brief note in the 'Connecting from workloads' section or FAQ about Lambda + relational databases and connection exhaustion: Lambda scales horizontally and each instance opens its own connection pool, which can exhaust database max_connections quickly. Mentioning RDS Proxy as a mitigation for Lambda workloads would be high-value practical context.

The Aurora Serverless v1 code example omits the `version` property. Since the source marks `version` as optional for v1 ('Usually managed by AWS automatically for serverless v1'), a one-sentence clarification that version is omitted intentionally would help readers distinguish this from v2 where version is required.

The 'Backups and maintenance' section discusses `preferredMaintenanceWindow` but does not mention the `deletionProtection` property alongside a note that you must deploy `deletionProtection: false` before deleting a stack — this is mentioned at the top of the section but could be reinforced with a code snippet showing the pattern, since it is a frequent operational gotcha.

The FAQ answer to 'How do I run database migrations with Stacktape?' references `local-script-with-bastion-tunneling` but does not explain when bastion tunneling is needed vs. not needed. A one-sentence distinction (only needed for scoping-workloads-in-vpc / vpc databases) would avoid confusion.

issues.statement: Aurora Serverless v2 bills per ACU-hour with no minimum instance commitment.
issues.reason: The phrase 'no minimum instance commitment' is potentially misleading because Aurora Serverless v2 does have a minimum capacity floor (minCapacity default 0.5 ACU). The source describes this as a configurable minimum, not zero. The intended meaning is 'no fixed instance size commitment' (as opposed to provisioned Aurora), which is accurate but could confuse readers into thinking there is zero baseline cost.
issues.suggestedFix: Rephrase to: 'Aurora Serverless v2 bills per ACU-hour starting from a configurable minimum (default 0.5 ACU), with no fixed instance size commitment.'

evidence.quote: minCapacity?: number; ... @default 0.5

issues.statement: The `mod` level for `log_statement` captures all data-modifying operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) in addition to DDL — useful for audit trails.
issues.reason: The source JSDoc for log_statement says: 'mod' (ddl + INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE). The draft adds 'useful for audit trails' which is reasonable general guidance but is the writer's opinion rather than source-grounded. This is a very minor point — the factual part about what 'mod' captures is accurate.
issues.suggestedFix: No change needed — the guidance is helpful and the factual claim about mod level is correctly sourced. The 'useful for audit trails' is reasonable advice.
