reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: All four engine variants (RDS, Aurora, Aurora Serverless v2, Serverless v1) get equal-depth treatment: working code example, instance/capacity guidance, tradeoffs, and a clear when-to-use signal. This is the most common shortcut that docs skip.

The <FeatureComparisonTable> engine overview near the top lets a first-time reader narrow the decision before reading four pages of engine-specific detail. The 'Best for' row gives a direct recommendation.

Aurora Serverless v2 accessibility constraint (vpc/scoping-workloads-in-vpc required) is flagged both in the Basic example <Info> callout and again in each Serverless v2/v1 section — readers cannot miss it.
The Credentials section carries a prominent <Warning> about the data-loss consequence of changing masterUserName post-deploy. This is a serious footgun and it's appropriately surfaced.
The connectTo section shows both the Stacktape config and the handler code side by side, giving a first-timer a complete end-to-end path without having to jump elsewhere.
The FAQ covers all three required question categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS use-case, comparison/choosing) and all 10 answers are 2–4 sentences, suitable for snippet extraction.
Pricing context near the top cites source-grounded approximations (~$12/month for db.t4g.micro, ~$35/month for db.t3.small) with the correct 'approximate' framing.
Alarms section leads with an opinionated recommendation ('at minimum, set up a database-free-storage alarm on every production database') instead of just listing options.
problems: The 'Supported versions' table header says 'Here are some current examples' but the table caption implies completeness. The page never tells the reader where to find the authoritative current list — the AWS documentation or the Stacktape Console. A reader creating a new stack may set a version that has since been deprecated.

The 'High availability and read replicas' section explains RDS multiAz, Aurora instances, and Aurora Serverless v2 readers in prose but the code example only shows RDS with multiAz + readReplicas. Aurora and Aurora Serverless v2 HA configurations are described without a dedicated example, leaving the reader to infer the config shape from the engine sections earlier in the page.

The Storage section states Aurora 'manages storage automatically and scales transparently up to 128 TB' but the source does not state this cap — it's a known AWS fact but is cited as if Stacktape documented it. This is likely to trigger a source-grounding flag.

The logging section is the longest prose section on the page. It enumerates every PostgreSQL and MySQL log option with default values, which belongs more in the API reference than in the guide layer. A busy reader trying to 'turn on slow query logging' has to wade through five options they don't need.
optionalImprovements: Add a one-sentence note to the 'Supported versions' table directing readers to the Stacktape Console or AWS docs for the canonical current version list, since engine versions change frequently and the table will go stale.
Add a working Aurora Serverless v2 example with serverlessReadersCount set to 1 in the 'High availability' section so readers get a concrete copy-pasteable config for Aurora HA, not just prose.

Trim the logging section to a quick-start recommendation (e.g., 'For production, set log_min_duration_statement: 1000 and log_statement: mod') and move the full option enumeration into an expandable callout or just defer to the API reference. The current depth is reference-level, not guide-level.

Replace the Aurora 128 TB storage ceiling claim with a hedged AWS-fact framing: 'Underneath, AWS Aurora manages storage automatically — the AWS-documented ceiling is 128 TB' — or remove the number and simply say 'Aurora storage scales automatically with no manual configuration'.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: All four engine families (RDS, Aurora, Aurora Serverless v2, Aurora Serverless v1) are covered with working code examples, per-variant decision guidance, and accurate defaults sourced from the type definitions.
Engine comparison FeatureComparisonTable is well-structured and correctly reflects source data: Aurora Serverless v1's pause-when-idle capability, v2's 0.5-ACU minimum, RDS's fixed-instance model.

Aurora Serverless accessibility-mode restriction (`vpc` or `scoping-workloads-in-vpc` only) is called out in two places — the Info callout in the basic example and the info block in the accessibility section — which is appropriate repetition for a common footgun.

Connectto env vars (`STP_MAIN_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING`, `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_HOST`, `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_PORT`, plus Aurora reader variants) match the authoritative `ResourceAccessProps` JSDoc exactly.

Alarm triggers table is complete and accurate: all six `RelationalDatabaseAlarmTrigger` variants (`database-cpu-utilization`, `database-free-memory`, `database-free-storage`, `database-connection-count`, `database-read-latency`, `database-write-latency`) are listed with correct threshold unit labels.

Production-oriented opinions are concrete and opinionated: `scoping-workloads-in-vpc` for production, `deletionProtection: true`, `automatedBackupRetentionDays` ≥ 7, storage alarm as a minimum requirement.
All ten FAQ questions cover the three required categories (Stacktape-specific, underlying AWS service use-case, and choosing/comparison), with accurate source-grounded answers.

No mandatory style violations: all Stacktape config examples are in `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}/>`, a single `<ApiReference definitionName='RelationalDatabaseProps' />` is present, no YAML config blocks, no StacktapeConfig-typed patterns.
problems: Connection pooling is completely absent. Lambda functions connecting to RDS/Aurora open a new database connection per invocation (or reuse one per container if the client is initialized outside the handler), but with many concurrent Lambdas this routinely exhausts the database's `max_connections`. This is the most common production issue teams hit after deploying Lambda+RDS. Not mentioning it — not even a callout to RDS Proxy — leaves the target reader without a critical operational signal.

The basic example uses `accessibilityMode: 'vpc'` while the accessibility section clearly recommends `scoping-workloads-in-vpc` for production. No note explains the deliberate inconsistency. A reader who copies the basic example for production will not get the recommended security posture.

The 'Supported versions' table uses static version strings ('16.11', '8.0.mysql_aurora.3.11.1', etc.) without noting they are examples — AWS regularly deprecates old versions and adds new ones. A reader relying on the table for a version that has since been deprecated will get a deployment error.

The `multiAz` property is introduced in the HA section prose and used in the RDS code example, but the Standard RDS subsection earlier does not mention it at all, creating an ordering disconnect for readers who scan the engine subsections before reaching the HA section.
optionalImprovements: Add a connection pooling callout or short paragraph in the 'Connecting from workloads' section. At minimum: explain that each Lambda container holds a single connection and concurrent Lambdas can exhaust `max_connections`; mention RDS Proxy as the managed AWS solution; recommend initializing the DB client outside the handler so connections are reused across warm invocations.

In the basic example, add a one-sentence comment or inline note explaining that `accessibilityMode: 'vpc'` is used here for simplicity and that production deployments should use `scoping-workloads-in-vpc` instead — prevents copy-paste of an insecure default.

Add a sentence to the 'Supported versions' section stating that the versions listed are examples only and current supported versions should be verified in the Stacktape Console or AWS documentation, since AWS adds and removes versions over time.

In the 'Standard RDS' subsection, mention `multiAz` on the primary instance briefly (one sentence) so readers scanning engine subsections see HA options per engine before reaching the dedicated HA section.

The `dbName` property exists on all Aurora engine types (default `defdb`) and on RDS engines, but the page only references it in the Serverless v1 example. A one-line note in the credentials or basic example section (or an explicit mention that the default initial database name is `defdb` for Aurora) would prevent confusion when users first connect and find an unexpected database name.

The `pauseAfterSeconds` behavior on Serverless v1 — that the *first connection after resume* times out and needs application-level retry — is mentioned but without a recommended retry strategy. Even a one-sentence note like 'configure your ORM or connection library with a retry-on-connect timeout of at least 60 seconds' would help.

The FAQ answer to 'How do I run database migrations?' mentions `local-script-with-bastion-tunneling` but omits that a bastion host resource must be included in the stack config. Adding a one-sentence qualifier avoids surprise for readers who try the bastion tunnel approach before deploying a bastion.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: All four engine families (RDS, Aurora, Aurora Serverless v2, Aurora Serverless v1) are covered at equal depth — each has a working code example, per-variant tradeoffs, cost context, and explicit when-to-use guidance. This is rare in generated docs.

The engine comparison table is fact-dense and citable: it captures scaling behavior, HA model, read replica support, pause-when-idle, starting cost, supported engines, and a best-for recommendation for each variant in a single scannable grid.
Concrete pricing numbers are present throughout and grounded in the source pricing data (~$12/month for db.t4g.micro, ~$35/month for db.t3.small Aurora) with correct '~' approximation framing.

Aurora Serverless VPC accessibility constraint is called out in an Info callout in both the Basic Example and the Engine Types section, and again in the Accessibility table — preventing a common deploy-time error.

The connectTo env-var claims are scoped precisely to ResourceAccessProps (CONNECTION_STRING, HOST, PORT) and the JDBC variants are correctly deferred to $ResourceParam() rather than invented as injected vars, following the source-grounding guidance.

FAQ has exactly 10 questions spanning all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (accessibility, env vars, Aurora Serverless VPC), AWS use-case (RDS cost, migration, backup/restore), and comparison questions (Aurora vs RDS, Serverless v2 vs standard).
All version numbers in the 'Supported versions' table are validated against versions.json (e.g., aurora-postgresql-serverless-v2 max is 16.11, not 17.x — correctly shown).
Practical application code example (pg client connection string) is included alongside the Stacktape config, bridging the gap between configuration and runtime usage.
The masterUserName warning (replacing it destroys data) is prominently placed in the Credentials section as a Warning callout — a high-severity operational gotcha surfaced where it matters.
The alarm section includes a concrete production recommendation ('At minimum, set up a database-free-storage alarm on every production database') rather than just listing alarm types.
problems: Minor pricing precision: the RDS instance size table shows ~$12/month for db.t4g.micro, but the source pricing file gives ~$11.52/month. Within the '~' qualifier range but slightly rounded up rather than down.

The 'Connecting from workloads' section mentions JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING as available via $ResourceParam() but does not clarify that scripts (local-script, bastion-script) do get STP_MAIN_DATABASE_JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING injected automatically via connectTo (per ScriptEnvProps.connectTo JSDoc). The distinction between workload connectTo and script connectTo is not surfaced, which could confuse readers running migrations via scripts.

The 'Supported versions' table lists the Aurora MySQL Serverless v2 row as showing only '8.0.mysql_aurora.3.11.1' — a single example version — without noting that multiple patch variants exist. This is accurate but may give the impression that MySQL Serverless v2 has very limited version support.

The 'Storage' section says 'Storage configuration applies to standard RDS engines only' but doesn't clarify that Aurora storage is also excluded from this section (not just Serverless). Minor ambiguity since Aurora is provisioned too.

The retentionDays property in the logging section is described as an integer range but the source actually defines it as a discrete union type (1 | 3 | 5 | 7 | 14 | 30 | 60 | 90 | ...). Using an unsupported value like 45 would silently fail — not calling this out leaves a latent gotcha.
optionalImprovements: In the 'Connecting from workloads' section, add a sentence clarifying that scripts (local-script, local-script-with-bastion-tunneling) receive additional auto-injected vars including STP_MAIN_DATABASE_JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING and STP_MAIN_DATABASE_READER_JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING via connectTo, which is useful for migration scripts that require JDBC URLs.

In the Logging section, note that retentionDays accepts only specific discrete values (1, 3, 5, 7, 14, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 365, 400, 545, 731, 1827, 3653) and that an unsupported integer will be rejected at deploy time. This prevents a confusing config error.

The instance size cost table (for standard RDS) uses three example rows. Adding a note that costs shown are us-east-1 on-demand single-AZ list prices before storage and I/O would match the source pricing file's disclaimer and help readers understand why their bill may differ.

In the 'Storage' section, explicitly mention that Aurora (including provisioned Aurora, not just Serverless) manages storage automatically — the current text says 'Aurora manages storage automatically' but only in contrast to 'standard RDS engines', which could leave readers wondering whether provisioned Aurora is included.

Consider adding a brief mention of the `dbName` property behavior: both RDS and Aurora engines accept a `dbName` property that names the initial database (defaults to 'defdb' for Aurora, configurable for RDS). This is a common question when connecting ORMs that expect a specific database name.

verifierResults.summary: This is an exceptionally well-grounded draft. Every major claim — defaults, property names, engine types, accessibility modes, alarm triggers, logging options, credential constraints, and pricing — aligns with the source files. The engine comparison table accurately reflects the four engine families. All cross-links point to valid routes in the navigation index. The connectTo env-var documentation correctly follows the ResourceAccessProps JSDoc. The FAQ covers 10 questions with a good mix of Stacktape-specific, AWS use-case, and comparison topics. Code examples use correct class names and valid engine versions. I found no high-severity factual issues and only minor items worth noting.

issues.statement: For PostgreSQL, `log_statement` can be `none`, `ddl` (CREATE/ALTER/DROP), `mod` (ddl + INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE), or `all`
issues.reason: The source JSDoc describes the `ddl` value as 'CREATE/ALTER' without mentioning DROP. The draft adds '/DROP'. While PostgreSQL DDL logging does include DROP statements (general PostgreSQL knowledge), the parenthetical expansion doesn't match the source text exactly. This is unlikely to mislead anyone — DDL always includes DROP — but it is technically not source-grounded.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to 'ddl (CREATE/ALTER/DROP and other DDL)' or match the source exactly: 'ddl (CREATE/ALTER)'.

evidence.quote: Which SQL statements to log: `none`, `ddl` (CREATE/ALTER), `mod` (ddl + INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE), `all`.

issues.statement: The `minCapacity` of `0.5` (the default) keeps costs low during idle periods
issues.reason: While 0.5 is correctly cited as the default for Aurora Serverless v2, the basic example prose could note that the default maxCapacity is 10, not the 4 shown in the example. The example sets maxCapacity: 4 and the prose explains the value, but a reader skimming may not realize 10 is the default. The Aurora Serverless v2 dedicated section later clarifies this, so impact is low.
issues.suggestedFix: Consider adding a parenthetical like '(default is 10)' when first mentioning maxCapacity in the basic example prose, or note the example deliberately caps it lower than the default.

evidence.quote:
Maximum ACUs (0.5-128 in 0.5 increments). Caps your scaling and cost.
   *
   * @default 10

positiveFindings: All four engine families (RDS, Aurora, Aurora Serverless v2, Aurora Serverless v1) are documented with equal depth — each gets a dedicated subsection, a working code example, tradeoff guidance, and cost context. The feature comparison table accurately summarizes differences.

Every default value cited in the draft matches the source @default annotations: masterUserName ('db_master_user'), automatedBackupRetentionDays (1), accessibilityMode ('internet'), minCapacity (0.5 for v2, 2 for v1), maxCapacity (10 for v2, 4 for v1), serverlessReadersCount (0), storage.initialSize (20), storage.maxSize (200), logging.retentionDays (90), log_min_duration_statement (10000), log_statement ('ddl'), server_audit_events (['QUERY_DDL']), long_query_time (10).

Pricing figures are well-grounded: RDS ~$12/month for db.t4g.micro matches the source's ~$11.52/month, Aurora ~$35/month for db.t3.small matches ~$35.28/month. All prices use '~' to acknowledge regional variation as required.

The connectTo environment variable documentation correctly follows the ResourceAccessProps JSDoc, listing CONNECTION_STRING, HOST, PORT (and READER_CONNECTION_STRING, READER_HOST for Aurora) without making negative claims about what is not injected. Additional parameters are correctly directed to $ResourceParam().

All six relational database alarm trigger types match the source: database-cpu-utilization, database-free-memory, database-free-storage, database-connection-count, database-read-latency, database-write-latency. Threshold property names (thresholdPercent, thresholdMB, thresholdSeconds, thresholdCount) are all correct.

The Aurora Serverless accessibility constraint ('only supports vpc or scoping-workloads-in-vpc') is correctly stated and consistently enforced across the basic example, the engine sections, and the Info boxes — matching the source JSDoc exactly.
Engine version examples (e.g., PostgreSQL 16.11 for Aurora Serverless v2) are all present in the versions.json source file for their respective engine types.

All cross-links resolve to valid routes in the navigation index. The ApiReference uses the correct definitionName 'RelationalDatabaseProps' matching the source type. The ReferenceableParams component uses the correct resource type 'relational-database'.
The FAQ contains exactly 10 Q&A pairs spanning Stacktape-specific questions, AWS use-case questions, and comparison questions — meeting the style guide requirement.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the relational-database type source, uses the correct ApiReference name, avoids forbidden hand-authored YAML config examples, and covers all major engine variants. The most important fixes are around over-specific unsupported behavior: certain environment variables, dev-mode support, migrations script syntax, and a few claims that read as Stacktape behavior but are not grounded in the provided sources.

issues.statement: For a relational database named `mainDatabase`, `connectTo` injects `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING`, `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_HOST`, and `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_PORT`. Aurora clusters also get `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_READER_CONNECTION_STRING` and `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_READER_HOST`. Additional parameters such as `jdbcConnectionString` and `dbName` are available via the `$ResourceParam()` directive.
issues.reason: The workload `connectTo` JSDoc for `ResourceAccessProps` only lists relational database env vars as `CONNECTION_STRING`, `HOST`, `PORT`, and for Aurora `READER_CONNECTION_STRING`, `READER_HOST`. `JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING` is listed for scripts in `ScriptEnvProps`, not for workload `connectTo`. The draft is careful not to say JDBC is injected for workloads, but the wording combines workload `connectTo` and script-style/env behavior and may mislead readers about where JDBC is injected. It also omits the source distinction that scripts have a different documented injection list.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the workload paragraph to the variables listed under `ResourceAccessProps`: `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING`, `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_HOST`, `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_PORT`, and for Aurora `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_READER_CONNECTION_STRING`, `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_READER_HOST`. Add a separate sentence: scripts document `JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING` injection, while `$ResourceParam()` exposes `jdbcConnectionString`, `dbName`, and replica-specific parameters.

evidence.quote:
**`RelationalDatabase`** — network access + connection details → `CONNECTION_STRING`, `HOST`, `PORT`.
   * Aurora also gets `READER_CONNECTION_STRING`, `READER_HOST`.

**`RelationalDatabase`**: `CONNECTION_STRING`, `JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING`, `HOST`, `PORT`. For Aurora clusters, `READER_CONNECTION_STRING`, `READER_JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING`, and `READER_HOST` are also included.

issues.statement: During [`stacktape dev`](/local-development/dev-mode-overview), relational databases run locally in Docker by default. This gives you a local database for development without deploying to AWS or incurring any AWS cost. Set `dev.remote` to `true` to connect to the deployed AWS database instead.
issues.reason: The relational database type exposes a `dev?: DevModeConfig` property, and `DevModeConfig` says databases run locally in Docker by default. However, the helper type `DevModeCapableResourceType` only includes `batch-job`, `multi-container-workload`, and `function`, which creates ambiguity about whether relational databases themselves are dev-mode capable in this source set. The draft states the behavior strongly for relational databases, including no AWS cost, without enough source support beyond a generic JSDoc sentence.
issues.suggestedFix: Write cautiously: “The `dev` property uses `DevModeConfig`, whose JSDoc says databases, Redis, and DynamoDB run locally in Docker by default and can use `remote: true` to connect to the deployed resource. Check the local databases page for current support details.” Avoid the unsupported “without deploying to AWS or incurring any AWS cost” claim unless another source confirms it.

evidence.quote: dev?: DevModeConfig;
type DevModeCapableResourceType = Subtype<StpResourceType, 'batch-job' | 'multi-container-workload' | 'function'>;

By default, databases, Redis, and DynamoDB run locally in Docker during dev mode.
   * Set to `true` to connect to the real deployed resource instead (must be deployed first).

issues.statement: Use [lifecycle hooks](/configuration/hooks-and-scripts) to run migrations after deploy. Define a `local-script` in the `scripts` section of your config with `connectTo` pointing to your database, then reference it in `hooks.afterDeploy`. Stacktape injects the connection string environment variable into your script automatically. For databases behind a VPC, use `local-script-with-bastion-tunneling` to tunnel through a bastion host.
issues.reason: The source supports scripts, hooks, `connectTo`, and `local-script-with-bastion-tunneling`, but the draft does not show that `connectTo` belongs in the script properties and that hooks reference scripts by `scriptName`. The claim is directionally correct but underspecified enough to be easy to configure incorrectly.
issues.suggestedFix: Revise the FAQ answer to explicitly name the source-grounded shapes: define a script of type `local-script` or `local-script-with-bastion-tunneling`, set the script’s `connectTo` list to include the database resource name, and reference the script from `hooks.afterDeploy` using `scriptName`. Keep the link to the migration recipe.

evidence.quote: scripts?: { [scriptName: string]: LocalScript | BastionScript | LocalScriptWithBastionTunneling };

interface ScriptEnvProps {
  /**
   * #### Connect To
   *
   * ---
   *
   * A list of resources the script needs to interact with. Stacktape automatically injects environment variables with connection details for each specified resource.

issues.statement: The connection string includes the username, password, host, port, and database name.
issues.reason: The source only says credentials are included in the auto-generated connection string and lists referenceable parameters such as host, port, and dbName. It does not explicitly define the full connection string contents. The claim is plausible for a database URL, but it is more specific than the provided Stacktape source supports.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow to: “The auto-generated connection string includes the database credentials and connection details.” Then point readers to `host`, `port`, `dbName`, and `jdbcConnectionString` in referenceable parameters for cases that need individual parts.

evidence.quote:
Included in the auto-generated connection string. Store the password in a Stacktape secret
   * to avoid exposing it in your config file.

type RelationalDatabaseReferencableParam =
  | 'host'
  | 'hosts'
  | 'connectionString'
  | 'jdbcConnectionString'
  | 'port'
  | 'dbName'

issues.statement: AWS automatically takes daily backups of your database. The `automatedBackupRetentionDays` property controls how many days to keep backups (default `1`, range 0–35). Set to `0` to disable automated backups (RDS only — Aurora always retains at least 1 day).
issues.reason: The source supports automated daily backups, the 0–35 range, default 1, and “Set to 0 to disable (RDS only).” It does not state that Aurora always retains at least 1 day. That may be true AWS behavior, but the sentence presents it as a Stacktape property constraint not grounded in the provided type source.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the source wording: “Set to `0` to disable automated backups for RDS engines. The source marks this as RDS-only; for Aurora engines, keep the retention value at `1` or higher.”

evidence.quote:
Days to keep automated daily backups (0-35). Set to 0 to disable (RDS only).
   *

issues.statement: Set `deletionProtection` to `true` on production databases to prevent accidental deletion. You must deploy with `deletionProtection: false` before you can delete the stack.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Covers all four engine families (RDS, Aurora, Aurora Serverless v2, Aurora Serverless v1) with dedicated code examples and per-variant tradeoff guidance — no variant is left as a one-liner mention.

The FeatureComparisonTable at the top of Engine types gives a first-time reader an instant decision matrix before they read any prose — this is exactly what the audience needs before choosing an engine.
Pricing context is accurate, tied to source (versions.json + price file), and annotated with '~' to acknowledge regional variation.

All connectTo env-var claims (`STP_MAIN_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING`, `HOST`, `PORT`, Aurora reader params, scripts-only JDBC vars) are correctly scoped to the right resource types and match the ResourceAccessProps and ScriptEnvProps JSDoc sources.

Property-value anchors are present throughout: every tunable property in code examples (instanceSize, minCapacity, maxCapacity, multiAz, pauseAfterSeconds, log_min_duration_statement) is explained in surrounding prose.
10-question FAQ spans all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS service behavior, choosing/comparison), with concise 2–4 sentence answers that stand alone as extractable snippets.
Application-code snippet showing `process.env.STP_MAIN_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING` with the pg client closes the loop from config to running code — essential for a first-timer.
Opinionated 'When NOT to use' section with concrete alternatives (DynamoDB, Redis, OpenSearch, MongoDB Atlas) helps readers who've arrived at the wrong page leave quickly and confidently.
The Aurora Serverless v1 Warning callout and the t-family burstable CPU throttling explanation are genuine 'gotchas' that save production incidents — not padding.
problems: The Basic example section and the Standard RDS sub-section use identical code. A first-time reader will encounter the same snippet twice within two scroll-lengths, which erodes trust and wastes space.

The 'Database name and port' H2 merges two independent concerns. 'Port' is a two-sentence topic and 'dbName' is a more substantial one; combining them makes the section hard to link to or scan for either topic individually.

The 'Supported versions' section lists version strings but provides no guidance on which version to pick (always choose the latest stable? stay one minor behind?). A first-timer will not know what '16.11 vs 17.7' means for their project.

The 'Connecting from workloads' section implies workloads always receive `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_PORT` injected via connectTo. The ResourceAccessProps JSDoc does list PORT for RelationalDatabase, so this is accurate — but the page doesn't mention that Aurora Serverless engines do not expose a configurable port property (stated elsewhere in the page, under 'Database name and port'), creating a slight inconsistency: the env var is injected but the port can't be changed for Serverless engines.

The long page structure means the 'Basic example' — the anchor for a first-timer — appears at H2 position 3 after two multi-paragraph prose sections. Low-AWS-experience readers may not reach it before bouncing.
optionalImprovements: Make the Basic example visually distinct from the Standard RDS example — either change the basic example to use Aurora Serverless v2 (the recommended engine) and note 'for a standard RDS example, see Engine types below', or add a one-line callout after the basic example pointing to the engine comparison before repeating the same snippet.
Split 'Database name and port' into two separate H2s — 'Database name' and 'Port' — or merge port coverage into the relevant engine-type sub-sections where it is already partially discussed.

Add one sentence at the start of the 'Supported versions' section advising readers to use the latest stable minor version within a given major (e.g., 'Use the most recent patch within your chosen major — e.g., 16.11 rather than 16.6 — to get the latest bug fixes'). The versions table answers 'what is valid' but not 'what should I choose'.

Consider adding a brief 'Connection pooling' note under 'Connecting from workloads' — RDS has a max-connections limit per instance class, and first-timers using Lambda or ECS with many concurrent tasks frequently hit it. A one-sentence callout to RDS Proxy or a pooler library (PgBouncer) would prevent a common production surprise.

The FAQ answer for 'How do I run database migrations' references `local-script-with-bastion-tunneling` — consider linking directly to the bastion-host and deployment-scripts pages from that answer since both are non-obvious resources for a first-timer.

Move or link the 'Dev mode' section earlier in the page (or reference it from the Basic example) — a first-timer deploying for the first time will likely hit local dev mode before they think about alarms or storage.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Answer-first paragraph is tight, self-contained, and accurately summarizes what the resource does and why it matters — meets the 40-75 word AEO target.

All four engine variants receive a working code example plus when-to-use decision guidance, per-variant cost context, and clear tradeoffs — strong union-type coverage discipline.

Exactly one `<ApiReference definitionName="RelationalDatabaseProps" />` placed at the bottom — correct and non-duplicated.
problems: The 'Basic example' section and the 'Standard RDS' subsection under 'Engine types' contain identical code (RdsEnginePostgres with db.t4g.micro). This makes the Standard RDS subsection feel like a repeat rather than adding new information. Readers scanning down from the basic example will not know why the same block appears again.

The 'Storage' section is placed after 'Connecting from workloads', but storage configuration is an RDS engine property and is logically part of the engine setup. Readers configuring an RDS instance will expect to see storage options before they see networking/connection content.

issues.statement: “Running out of storage causes the database to become read-only and can be difficult to recover from quickly.”
issues.reason: The source supports configuring free-storage alarms and RDS storage autoscaling, but it does not state this failure behavior. This may be a general AWS/RDS operational fact, but it is phrased as a direct outcome for Stacktape databases and could be over-specific without source support.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the recommendation while avoiding the precise failure claim: “At minimum, set up a `database-free-storage` alarm on every production RDS database so you can react before writes are affected.”

evidence.quote: Storage configuration. Auto-scales up when free space is low.

issues.reason: The provided source states “Aurora Serverless engines only support `vpc` or `scoping-workloads-in-vpc`,” which supports the claim. No issue.

issues.statement: “Aurora manages storage automatically and scales transparently up to 128 TB.”
issues.reason: The source supports that storage configuration applies only to RDS by omission and defines `storage` only on `RdsEngineProperties`, but it does not mention Aurora’s 128 TB storage limit. This is general AWS knowledge, so it is not publication-blocking, but the exact numeric limit is not grounded in the provided Stacktape sources.
issues.suggestedFix: Either remove the number or phrase it as AWS background: “Storage configuration applies to standard RDS engines only; Aurora manages storage automatically at the AWS service layer.”

issues.statement: The draft says “Aurora Serverless v1 manages its version automatically — the `version` property is optional.”
issues.reason: The source says the v1 `version` property is optional and “usually managed by AWS automatically.” The draft removes “usually,” making the behavior more absolute than the source.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: “Aurora Serverless v1 usually has its engine version managed by AWS automatically; the `version` property is optional.”
